1:
The book's history of the kingdom of Judah opens on the very day the united monarchy breaks in two, and the witnesses read the scene at Shechem as at once a fracture and a fulfilment. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the frame: having told of the revolt of the ten tribes from the divinely chosen house of David, the chronicler now confines himself to Judah, and narrates its history under Rehoboam, Abijah, Asa, and Jehoshaphat far more fully than the older book of Kings, which treats the southern kingdom only briefly. His purpose throughout, they urge, is to show, according to the varying attitude of the kings of David's house toward the LORD and His law, how the fortunes of Judah rose and fell, so that the schism recorded here is not mere politics but the opening of a sustained theological argument.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the muster at Shechem into a cluster of hard lessons. The wisest and best cannot please everybody: Solomon had enriched and advanced his kingdom, yet either indiscreetly burdened his people with taxes and services, or gave colour for them to think so, for no man is perfectly wise, and it was likely when Solomon had declined from God that his wisdom failed him. Turbulent and ungrateful spirits will find fault with the government where they have little reason: had they not peace and plenty in Solomon's time? And chiefly, Henry says, many ruin themselves by trampling on their inferiors; Rehoboam wore his father's crown but wanted his father's brains, and, being a far different man, ought to have taken far different measures. He commends against the king the example of Job, who despised not the cause of his manservant when they argued with him, at Job 31:13, but heard them patiently and returned a soft answer.

Both Clarke and Ellicott fix the historical detail. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke summarizes the whole chapter, how the people petition for relief, Rehoboam rejects the aged counsellors for the young men, ten tribes revolt, Hadoram is stoned, and the king barely escapes, and notes that the account is almost word for word the same as 1 Kings 12:1-19, to which he refers the reader. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, attentive to the letter, marks that "to Shechem" is here written with the old accusative ending where Kings has the plain form, and that "were come" stands in the plural where Kings has the singular, small tokens of the chronicler's independent hand even in a passage he copies closely. The witnesses do not divide: Keil and Delitzsch give the design, Henry the moral, Clarke and Ellicott the frame and the philology, and together they open the tragedy of a kingdom torn by a king's folly and by God's determinate counsel.
2:
The narrative pauses to bring Jeroboam back from exile, and the witnesses attend to the awkward seam the chronicler leaves where he has abridged his source. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the clause "who was in Egypt" as really a parenthesis: "And it came to pass, when Jeroboam the son of Nebat heard (now he was in Egypt, whither he had fled from the face of Solomon the king), that Jeroboam returned from Egypt." The chronicler, he observes, has omitted to say that Jeroboam was still in Egypt, the word odennu that Kings supplies, precisely because he has nowhere alluded to the flight thither, the whole story of which stands in the older record at 1 Kings 11:26-40. Where Kings continues the parenthesis with "and Jeroboam dwelt in Egypt," the chronicler writes "returned out of Egypt," and Ellicott notes the delicate point that "dwelt" and "returned" are written with the very same consonants in Hebrew, the difference being one of pointing only, so that a reader must decide by the sense which the writer intended.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, simply carry the narrative forward as they reproduce it: Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who had fled from Solomon and remained in Egypt, hears of the gathering at Shechem and comes back, and the people send and call him to be their spokesman before the new king. Their interest lies less in the philology than in the movement of events, the exile summoned home at the very hour when the northern tribes are looking for a leader against the house of David.

Between the two witnesses the verse yields both its difficulty and its drift. The difficulty is the chronicler's habit of compression: having passed over Jeroboam's rebellion under Solomon and his flight into Egypt, he must reintroduce the man abruptly, and the seam shows in the clumsy parenthesis Ellicott unravels. The drift is ominous. The same Jeroboam whom Ahijah's prophecy had marked out for the throne of the ten tribes now steps back onto the stage, and the reader who knows the older history feels the gathering of forces the young king cannot see. The chronicler, writing long after both kingdoms had risen and fallen, does not linger over Jeroboam's past sins; he needs only to set the man in place, so that when the assembly speaks in the next verse it speaks with a practised rebel at its head. What Ellicott recovers from the letter, a returning exile and a text lightly scarred by abridgement, Keil and Delitzsch read as the quiet turning of the wheel by which Solomon's kingdom will be rent.
3:
A single verse carries the assembly into speech, and with only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attending closely to it, the reading is spare but exact. "And they sent and called him" means, Ellicott explains, that they summoned Jeroboam to the assembly gathered at Shechem, and he compares the fuller notice of the older history at 1 Kings 12:20, where the recall of the exile and his elevation before the congregation are told at greater length. Where Kings writes "the assembly of Israel," the chronicler shortens the phrase to "all Israel," dropping the word "assembly"; and where Kings has the plural "came," the chronicler writes the singular, small marks, once again, of a hand that copies the older record while trimming and smoothing it as it goes.

The bare grammar carries more weight than its brevity suggests. The place itself is charged: Shechem, in the hill-country of Ephraim, was the ancient seat of covenant and assembly, where Joshua had once gathered the tribes to renew their oath to the LORD; and it is there, on that old ground of national unity, that the nation now meets to break in two. The people do not merely receive Jeroboam; they "send and call" him, deliberately fetching the one man whose history made him the natural voice of their grievance. He who had fled Solomon's face returns at the nation's summons, and "all Israel," the northern tribes assembled to crown a king, come with him to stand before Rehoboam. The chronicler's clipped "all Israel" has its own force: it is the whole body of the ten tribes, not a faction, that advances to press its demand, so that what follows is no palace quarrel but a national confrontation.

Ellicott's philological care, spare as it is here, belongs to the argument the whole chapter rewards. The differences between this account and the older one are not contradictions but the ordinary traces of transmission and abridgement: a dropped noun, a shifted number, a verb in the singular where the source had the plural. The chronicler is re-telling a story his readers already knew from Kings, and he tells it faithfully, condensing where he may without altering the substance. So the verse does its plain work, the exile summoned, the tribes gathered, the spokesman set in place, and readies the confrontation that will break the kingdom. The assembly that came to make Rehoboam king at Shechem will, before the chapter closes, send him fleeing to Jerusalem; and the first step toward that rupture is taken here, quietly, as a whole nation and its recalled leader come forward together to speak.
4:
The people's demand is laid down in a single sentence, and the two witnesses divide it between the letter and the cause. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the wording against the older text. Where the common version reads "made grievous" and "ease thou," the Hebrew is rather "made hard" and "lighten"; and the opening "now therefore" he renders simply "and now." Here the chronicler diverges pointedly from his source: Kings, with the Syriac, reads "and thou now" (w'attah attah), an assonance the chronicler has deliberately avoided, though, Ellicott notes, at the expense of the proper emphasis, which in the older phrasing falls on the word thou. Some Hebrew manuscripts, with the Vulgate and Arabic, restore the "and thou"; and Ellicott compares the parallel construction a few verses on, where the young men coach the king to say "and thou, lighten it" at v.10. The point of the demand, in the older emphasis, is personal: thou, the new king, unlike thy father, lighten our load.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reaches past the grammar to the root of the grievance. Why was the yoke grievous at all? Because, he suggests, when Solomon had declined from God, God left him to himself to act thus impolitically, the burdensome taxes and forced service being the fruit of a wisdom that failed once its owner had forsaken his LORD. It is a shrewd theological reading of the complaint: the people blame the yoke, but the yoke itself was the visible mark of a great king's spiritual decline, so that Rehoboam inherits not only a heavy administration but the consequences of his father's apostasy.

The two witnesses meet without friction. Ellicott secures what the tribes actually said and how the chronicler shaped their saying; Wesley explains how the burden they resent came to be laid on them in the first place. Between them the verse stands as a fair and even moderate petition, "ease thou somewhat the grievous servitude, and we will serve thee," an offer of loyalty in exchange for relief, which a wiser king would have seized at once. The tragedy the chapter will unfold is that a request so reasonable, framed with the promise of willing service, should be met with the swagger of the next verses; and that the yoke Solomon's declension had fastened on Israel should, through his son's folly, become the very thing that tears the ten tribes away for ever. What the people asked was not liberty from all rule but relief from an excess of it, and they pledged obedience in the same breath; a king with his father's wisdom would have read in the very moderation of the demand how easily the whole nation might be kept.
5:
Rehoboam buys time, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the lone close reader of the verse, fixes both the idiom and a small gap in the text. The Hebrew, he notes, runs literally "Yet three days, and return unto me," and he suspects that the imperative "go ye," which the parallel in Kings supplies before these words, has fallen out of the chronicler's text, for the Septuagint, the Syriac, and the Arabic all preserve it. The closing clause, "and the people departed," he marks as singular in the chronicler where Kings has the plural, and he draws a pointed contrast with the opening of the chapter, where the verb of gathering stood in the plural at v.1; the assembly that came as a many now departs, grammatically, as one.

The delay itself is the verse's substance, and it cuts two ways. On the one hand a three-day pause is prudent: a young king faced with a national ultimatum does well not to answer in haste, but to take counsel, as the following verses show him doing, first with the old men and then with his agemates. The very phrase "come again unto me after three days" is a small courtesy, a promise to receive the people once more rather than a curt dismissal. On the other hand the interval is the very space in which the fatal choice is made, the room Rehoboam gives himself to weigh advice becoming the room in which he rejects the good and embraces the ruinous. The people, dismissed for three days, go home carrying their reasonable petition and their offer of service, and return to hear it thrown back in threats.

Ellicott's textual care, slight as it appears, keeps the reader close to the actual words the chronicler wrote and to the ways his copy differs from the older one, a missing imperative here, a singular verb there. Such variants are not blemishes but evidence: they show a later writer handing on a familiar narrative with a scribe's fidelity and an editor's economy, trimming a word, shifting a number, yet never touching the course of the story. The three days will pass; the assembly will reconvene at the appointed time; and the hinge of the whole tragedy, a king's answer, turns quietly in the little clause that sends the people away to wait. What Ellicott recovers from the grammar, the narrative confirms: a nation held three days in suspense between a petition offered in good faith and an answer that will drive it into revolt.
6:
With the people gone, the king turns first to the elders, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the sole voice on the verse, attends to the formulas of counsel. "Before Solomon," he notes, is here the phrase liphne Shelomoh, the common later expression standing for the older eth-pene Shelomoh of Kings, the men who "had stood before Solomon," that is, who had served in the presence of the great king and knew his mind and his methods. And the clause "to return answer to this people" he renders more literally "to return to this people a word," where Kings has the fuller double-accusative construction "to return this people a word," a construction, he observes, that the chronicler does preserve a little further on at v.9, so that the variation is one of style within the same writer, not of substance.

The men Rehoboam consults are the verse's real weight. They are the counsellors of his father's court, seasoned in the government of a great and prosperous reign, and their standing "before Solomon while he yet lived" marks them as the repository of a hard-won political wisdom. That the young king takes counsel with them first is to his credit; the tragedy is not that he failed to ask, but that he asked and would not hear. The verse frames the choice that governs the whole chapter: two bodies of advisers, the old and the young, set before a king who must decide whose voice shall shape his answer to a restive nation. The elders' turn comes first, and their advice, given in the next verse, will be the wiser, gentleness that would have secured the throne.

Ellicott's philological notes, spare as they are, keep the reader aware that the chronicler is retelling a story already told, in his own later idiom, faithful to the sense while free with the phrasing. The formulas he traces, "before Solomon," "to return a word," are the ordinary furniture of the older narrative, lightly modernized. But behind the grammar stands a scene of genuine moral gravity: a kingdom's future hanging on which counsel a proud young man will heed. The old men who had stood before Solomon are about to counsel his son toward the very kindness that would keep the ten tribes loyal; and the king who summoned them is about to turn from them to the flatterers of his own generation, with the loss of a nation as the price of his preference.
7:
The elders give their answer, and it is all gentleness, and the two witnesses admire both its wording and its wisdom. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds the chronicler's version a free paraphrase of the older text. Where Kings reads "If to-day thou become a servant to this people and serve them," the chronicler writes simply "If thou be kind to this people," softening words that may have seemed to the later editor unfitting when spoken of a king, though, Ellicott notes, the original phrasing forms a pointed antithesis with the promise that closes the verse: become their servant to-day, and "they will be thy servants for ever." The clause "and please them" he renders "be propitious to them, receive them graciously," the Hebrew ratsah being the word used of a gracious reception at Genesis 33:10, where Kings has instead the plainer "answer them."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the counsel's abiding principle. "Moderate counsels are generally best," he says; "gentleness will do what violence will not do. Good words cost nothing but a little self-denial, and yet they purchase great things." It is the whole philosophy of the elders in a sentence: a soft answer, a little condescension, a few kind words, and the loyalty of a nation is bought at no cost but the swallowing of a proud man's pride. The old men understand what the young king will not, that authority is most secure when it stoops, and that a throne is held more firmly by service than by threat.

The witnesses do not divide; they complete each other. Ellicott shows how the chronicler has recast the counsel, trading the striking paradox of the servant-king for a smoother "be kind," and recovering the graciousness of the verb behind "please them"; Wesley presses the timeless lesson the counsel carries. Together they set the elders' advice in its full worth, so that the reader may measure exactly what Rehoboam threw away. Here was the road to a united and grateful kingdom, kindness answered with lifelong allegiance, laid plainly before the king by the men who had governed at his father's side. The next verse will show him turning from it; and the pity of the chapter is sharpened by the reasonableness of the very counsel he rejects, a counsel that asked of him only that he speak good words to a people ready to serve him for ever. It is a counsel, moreover, that costs the king nothing he could not spare and gains him everything he stands to lose, so that its rejection is not merely imprudent but perverse.
8:
The verse names the fatal turn, the king forsaking the elders for his own generation, and though only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over its letter, his single observation opens onto the chronicler's whole manner. In the clause "the young men that were brought up with him, that stood before him," Ellicott notes, the chronicler has quietly omitted a redundant expression, the relative particle that the older text carried, one more instance of the compression that marks his retelling throughout, trimming a word the sense can spare while leaving the substance untouched.

The substance here needs no philology to feel its weight. Rehoboam had asked the old men and received the wisest of answers at v.7; now he "forsook the counsel which the old men gave him." The verb is deliberate: not that he weighed it and found it wanting, but that he abandoned it, thrust it from him because it did not flatter his sense of his own greatness. And he turns instead to "the young men that were brought up with him," the companions of his own upbringing, his agemates and courtiers, men who had grown up in the luxury of Solomon's palace and knew nothing of the labour by which a kingdom is held. They "stood before him" as the elders had stood before his father, but they stood as flatterers, not as counsellors, and their advice in the coming verses will be all bravado.

The tragedy the verse records is the perennial one of a ruler who seeks counsel only to have his own will confirmed. The elders offered Rehoboam the hard wisdom of experience, stoop, and be served for ever; his peers will offer him the easy wisdom of pride, threaten, and be feared. That he "took counsel" with the young after hearing the old makes his choice the more culpable: the better way had been shown him and he refused it. Ellicott's small note that the chronicler pared away a redundant word is itself a quiet emblem of the scene, for the chronicler, retelling a story his readers knew, keeps only what matters and lets the folly stand in its stark outline, the king who forsook the counsel of the aged, and turned to the men of his own age, and so began the ruin of his house. What the letter loses in a dropped particle, the narrative gains in force: the whole disaster of the divided kingdom is contained in this one verb, "he forsook."
9:
Rehoboam puts the people's demand to his younger counsellors, and here the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott has almost nothing to correct: "the verse agrees with Kings to the letter," he observes, the chronicler reproducing his source word for word at 1 Kings 12:9. It is a small but telling datum, for the chapter is full of the chronicler's minor divergences, dropped particles and shifted numbers and modernized idioms, and when a verse comes down identical to the older record, the very sameness is worth the noting: it shows how closely, where he does not choose to abridge, the writer keeps to the text he has received.

The verse itself is the king rehearsing the crisis to his peers. He lays before them exactly what the people had said, "Ease somewhat the yoke that thy father did put upon us," the demand first spoken at v.4 and now repeated in the king's own mouth, as if to invite the answer he already half desires. There is something revealing in the way he frames the question. He does not ask the young men whether the people's request is just, nor whether his father's yoke was in fact too heavy; he asks only "What advice give ye that we may return answer," the matter already reduced, in his mind, to a contest of wills between the throne and the tribes. The very repetition of the people's words is telling: the same demand that the elders had answered with counsels of mercy is now set before the young men, and the king waits to hear it answered with the counsels of pride.

Ellicott's terse note belongs to the same patient method that governs his whole reading of the chapter. Where the chronicler diverges, the grammatical-historical eye marks the divergence and weighs its cause; where he agrees, the agreement is registered too, as evidence of the fidelity with which the older narrative has been transmitted. The identity of this verse with its parallel is a quiet witness against the notion that the chronicler freely invented or recast his history: here, at the pivot of the story, he sets down the king's words precisely as they stood in the record before him. And those words carry the reader to the brink. Rehoboam has heard the elders and turned from them; he now hands the same question to the men whose answer he prefers, and the next verse will give their reply, the swaggering counsel of the little finger and the loins, on which the fate of the kingdom will turn.
10:
Now the young men answer, and their counsel is pure bravado, "My little finger shall be thicker than my father's loins," a boast the two witnesses illumine from the versions and the grammar. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reaches for the Targum's paraphrase, which unfolds the swaggering image into its plain meaning: "My weakness shall be stronger than the might of my father." The little finger against the loins is a figure of contempt, the least part of the son outmatching the whole strength of the father, and the Targumist, refusing to leave the metaphor to speak for itself, spells out the arrogance the young men mean to put in Rehoboam's mouth.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works over the wording with his usual care. The chronicler's "spake unto him" reads in the Hebrew "with him," which Ellicott judges probably a mistaken repetition, since Kings and the Septuagint have the smoother "unto him" at 1 Kings 12:10, though the Syriac agrees with the chronicler. The people's request "make thou it somewhat lighter for us" he renders literally "and thou lighten from upon us," praising the Septuagint's crisp rendering of the phrase. And on the famous image he corrects the printers: the word "finger" should not be set in italics as though supplied, for the single Hebrew word qoten means precisely "little finger," so the boast is not of some unnamed part but of the very least of the king's members, that it shall outweigh his father's strength.

The witnesses do not contend; they light the verse from two sides. Clarke, through the Targum, gives the sense of the taunt, the son's weakness greater than the father's might; Ellicott, through the grammar and the versions, secures its exact words and clears away a false italic. Between them the young men's counsel stands in its full folly: they would have the new king open his reign not with the gracious words the elders urged but with a sneer, meeting a nation's reasonable petition with the crude arithmetic of the little finger and the loins. It is counsel perfectly suited to a proud young man's vanity and perfectly calculated to lose him his people. The coaching runs on into the next verse, where the boast is spelled out in whips and scorpions; but already here, in the image of the overmatching little finger, the tone is set that will drive ten tribes to their tents. The young men, it seems, have mistaken insolence for majesty, and would have their king purchase the fear of his subjects at the price of their love.
11:
The young men's coaching reaches its climax in a threat, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the one witness to weigh the verse, renders its opening more literally than the common version: not "For whereas my father put a heavy yoke upon you," but "And now, my father... and I, I will add to your yoke," the emphatic "and I" setting the son over against the father in a boast of harsher rule. The instruments of that rule he gives their definite force, "the whips... the scorpions," and he notes that the whole saying, threat and figure alike, stands in the older text of Kings, and recurs almost unchanged when Rehoboam actually delivers it at v.14. The chronicler, that is, records the counsel here and its execution there in nearly identical words, so that the reader hears the fatal sentence twice, once rehearsed by the flatterers, once pronounced by the king.

The threat itself repays a moment's weight. "Scorpions," by the common understanding, were no mere metaphor but whips armed with points or knots, lashes made to tear rather than merely to sting, so that the young men counsel not only more of the same burden but a crueller kind of it, an escalation from the sharp to the savage. Where the elders had urged good words, the peers urge the language of the overseer's lash; where the people had asked for a lighter yoke, they are to be promised a heavier, laid on with instruments of torment. It is the rhetoric of a court that has never had to hold a kingdom together, mistaking cruelty for strength.

Ellicott's care to trace the saying through both the older record and its second occurrence in this very chapter belongs to his steady interest in how the chronicler handles his source. The words are not the chronicler's invention; they are copied from Kings, and copied so faithfully that the counsel and its fulfilment answer each other syllable for syllable. That doubling is itself a kind of judgment: the boast the young men put in the king's mouth becomes, three verses later, the very speech by which he loses the ten tribes. The whip and the scorpion, threatened here in the privacy of the council, will be flung in the face of a waiting nation; and the figure of the little finger, swollen now into lashes of torment, will do exactly what gentleness would have prevented, drive Israel away from the house of David for ever.
12:
The three days end and the assembly returns, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers from the coming confrontation a cluster of grave lessons on providence and human folly. First, that in a public ferment violent proceedings only make bad worse: rough answers, such as Rehoboam is about to give, do but stir up anger and bring oil to the flames, and the pilot has need to steer steadily in a storm. Second, and deepest, that whatever the devices and designs of men, God is by all of them doing His own work and fulfilling His word, no iota of which shall fall to the ground. The cause of the king's obstinacy, Henry insists, was of God, that He might perform the word He had spoken by Ahijah, as the next verse will declare at v.15, though this in no way excuses Rehoboam's folly or lessens the guilt of his haughtiness, that God was pleased to serve His own ends by it.

Henry draws two further lessons from the wreck about to fall. Worldly wealth, honour, and dominion are uncertain things: Solomon reigned over all Israel and seemed to have secured the monarchy to his house for ages, yet he is scarcely cold in his grave before ten of the twelve tribes finally revolt from his son, crying "What portion have we in David?" at v.16, as many still cast off the government of Christ, saying, "We will not have this man to reign over us." And God often visits the iniquities of the fathers upon the children: Solomon forsook God, and not he but his son after him is forsaken by the greater part of his people. Yet even here, Henry adds, when God fulfils His threatenings He takes care that His promises do not fall to the ground; for David's sake two tribes are preserved to the house, the covenant chastised but not broken, according to the pledge of Psalm 89:31-34.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds only that the verse stands "literatim as Kings," word for word with the older record, the chronicler copying the return of the assembly exactly as he found it. The two witnesses do not divide. Ellicott marks the fidelity of the text; Henry reads in the event the whole theology of the schism: a foolish king freely choosing his ruin, and a sovereign God working through that free folly to keep His ancient word to Jeroboam and His ancient promise to David at once. The reader is thus prepared to hold two truths together as the answer falls, that Rehoboam is wholly to blame, and that not one thread of the outcome lies outside the hand of God.
13:
The answer falls, and it is rough, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the small ways the chronicler's report of it differs from the older one at 1 Kings 12:13. Where Kings says the king answered "the people," the chronicler writes simply "them"; the word rendered "roughly" he glosses more exactly as "hardly," harshly; and where the chronicler names the king a second time, "and king Rehoboam forsook the counsel of the old men," the older text omits the name but adds the clause "that they counselled him," rounding out the phrase the chronicler leaves bare. These are the ordinary variations of a faithful retelling: a pronoun for a noun, a name inserted, a trailing clause dropped, none of them touching the sense.

The sense is stark enough without embellishment. The king "answered them hardly," met a nation's petition with harshness, and in the same breath "forsook the counsel of the old men," the very counsel of gentleness that would have held the kingdom together. The verse is the hinge of the tragedy stated twice over, once as manner and once as substance: the roughness of the answer and the rejection of the wise. What the earlier verse had recorded as an inward turning, the king taking counsel with the young rather than the old, is now made public and irrevocable in the harsh words flung at the waiting assembly.

Ellicott's philological patience keeps the reader close to the letter, and the letter here is worth keeping close, for the difference between "hardly" and the softer "roughly" of the common version sharpens the picture: this was not bluntness but harshness, the deliberate cruelty the young men had coached. The chronicler, retelling a story his readers knew from Kings, sets it down with his customary economy, trimming here, naming there, but he does not soften the verdict. Rehoboam forsook the counsel of the old men. The elders who had stood before Solomon and urged their king to stoop and be served for ever are dismissed in a single clause, and the harsh answer that seals the schism is delivered. All that remains is for the assembly to hear the actual words, the whips and the scorpions of the next verse, and to answer folly with revolt. The rupture that will divide the people of God for centuries is contained in this brief notice of a king who answered hardly and turned his back on wisdom.
14:
The king speaks the young men's script, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott devotes his note to a genuine textual crux buried in the boast, comparing the parallel at 1 Kings 12:14. The chronicler's "answered them" he renders "and spake unto them," and "advice" he gives as "counsel," small matters of diction. But the phrase "My father made your yoke heavy" hides a real divergence in the manuscripts: the Targum and a large number of Hebrew copies read instead "I will make heavy," turning the clause from a statement about Solomon into a fresh threat from Rehoboam. Ellicott judges this a mere error, arising from the fusion of the two Hebrew words abi hikhbid, "my father made heavy," into the single ahbid, "I will make heavy"; and he observes that all the ancient versions support the received reading, "my father made your yoke heavy." The closing "thereto" he expands from the older text as "to your yoke."

The variant is worth Ellicott's trouble, for the two readings give subtly different portraits of the king. In the received text Rehoboam contrasts himself with his father as one who will exceed him, "My father made your yoke heavy, but I will add thereto," a boast of surpassing severity; in the corrupted reading he claims even the past harshness as his own. Either way the substance is the same brutal escalation the peers had coached: the father's whips answered by the son's scorpions, the heavy yoke made heavier. The king has taken the counsel of the young men entire, and speaks it now, in the hearing of the assembled tribes, as his considered royal policy.

Ellicott's untangling of abi hikhbid from ahbid is a small masterpiece of the grammatical-historical method, a whole variant reading traced to two words run together by a copyist's eye, and adjudicated by the weight of the versions. But the human meaning stands clear above the philology. This is the speech that breaks the kingdom. The gracious words the elders had urged are utterly discarded; in their place the king flings a threat of torment at a people who had asked only for relief and promised, in return, their loyal service. The whips and scorpions, rehearsed in the council chamber, are now hurled in the open assembly; and the next verse will name the hidden hand that let the king's folly run its course, that an ancient prophecy might be fulfilled.
15:
The narrator now lifts the veil from the whole disaster: "the king hearkened not unto the people, for the cause was of God," and the two witnesses probe that startling clause where free folly and divine purpose meet. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the Targum's careful gloss, which will not let the words imply that God authored the king's sin: "For there was an occasion Divinely given." The turn of events was of God's providing, an occasion He supplied; the folly by which the occasion was seized remained Rehoboam's own.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the philology toward the same sense. "The cause was of God" he renders "it was brought about by God," and, more literally, "it was a turn or turning-point of events from with God," the Hebrew nesibbah answering to the sibbah of the older text, both words isolated in the Old Testament, the latter being the ordinary Rabbinic term for "cause," as in the phrase sibbath sibboth, cause of causes. The force of the word is a hinge, a turning: God so ordered the swing of events that the king's refusal served a purpose beyond the king's knowing. And that purpose the verse names, "that the LORD might perform his word" spoken by Ahijah the Shilonite to Jeroboam, the prophecy that first announced the tearing of ten tribes from Solomon's house at 1 Kings 11:29. Ellicott notes that the chronicler here does not deviate from Kings, even though he has nowhere recorded Ahijah's prophecy himself, referring the reader only obliquely to it, as at 2 Chronicles 9:29.

The witnesses converge on the mystery without dissolving it. Clarke, through the Targum, guards the divine justice: God gave the occasion, He did not work the wickedness. Ellicott, through the grammar, fixes the sense of the turning-point and roots it in Ahijah's word. Between them the verse states the chapter's deepest theme, that the same schism which is Rehoboam's guilt is God's determinate counsel, the king freely choosing the ruin by which the LORD fulfils an ancient sentence on the house of David. The reasonable petition refused, the harsh answer given, the tribes about to depart: all of it is at once the fruit of a proud man's folly and the accomplishment of a prophecy spoken years before to the very exile who now stands at the assembly's head. Human freedom and divine decree run together in a single sentence, and neither cancels the other.
16:
The break comes, and the tribes renounce the house of David in a cry of secession, "What portion have we in David?... To your tents, O Israel," which the two witnesses read for its grammar and its grief. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks how the chronicler reshapes the opening: where Kings has the plain "And all Israel saw," the chronicler writes "Now all Israel had seen," making a fresh start of the sentence, though the very word "saw" is missing from many Hebrew manuscripts and from the Septuagint, Vulgate, and Targum. "Answered the king," he notes, is literally "returned the king a word." On the rallying-cry "every man to your tents, O Israel," Ellicott finds the word "man" probably spurious, an accidental repetition of the letters of the preceding name Jesse, Yishai in the Hebrew, so that the older text at 1 Kings 12:16, the Septuagint, and the Vulgate read simply "to thy tents, O Israel." And the closing taunt, "David, see to thine own house," he glosses plainly: look now to Judah, thine own tribe, and to it alone, the Vulgate's pasce domum tuam, tend thine own house.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on that same closing phrase to draw the chapter's political moral. "See to thine own house," and Wesley reflects that when public affairs are in a ferment, violent proceedings only make ill worse; many have been driven to the mischief they never intended by being too severely dealt with. The secession, in his reading, is a warning to every ruler: harshness does not cow a restive people but hardens them, and the tribes who might have been kept by a soft answer are flung into open revolt by a hard one.

The witnesses meet on the pathos of the moment. Ellicott recovers the exact words of the rupture and clears away a spurious "man"; Wesley reads in those words the bitter fruit of Rehoboam's severity. "What portion have we in David?" the tribes disown the dynasty their fathers had crowned, the same cry with which rebellion had once flared in the days of David himself, and turn homeward, leaving the son of Solomon a king over Judah alone. It is the visible undoing of the united monarchy, a whole people walking away from the throne at Shechem; and the chronicler, writing centuries later, sets down their words of parting exactly as the older record preserved them, that his readers might see how a kingdom was lost for want of a few gracious words.
18:
The rupture turns bloody, and the two witnesses attend to the murdered officer and the fleeing king. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains, on the authority of Jarchi, why the tribes turned their stones on Hadoram: it was when he endeavoured to collect the tribute that Solomon had imposed on them. The man was the living symbol of the very yoke they had begged to have lightened, and Rehoboam's sending of him, the officer of forced labour, into the midst of a people already in revolt was folly heaped on folly; they answered the tax-gatherer with the stones due to the policy he embodied. That the king should respond to a bloodless secession by dispatching the very minister of the burden, rather than an envoy of peace, shows how completely he had misread the temper of the tribes, and how far the counsel of the young men had carried him.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the names and terms. "Hadoram" appears in Kings as "Adoram" and in the Vatican Septuagint as "Adoniram," the same high official, he suggests, comparing the roll of Solomon's ministers at 1 Kings 4:6. "The tribute" he renders more precisely "the levy," the body of men conscripted for the king's forced labour; where the chronicler writes "the children of Israel," Kings has "all Israel." "Made speed" he gives as "had made speed," and "his chariot" as "the royal chariot," the king scrambling, undignified, into his state carriage to save his life.

The witnesses do not divide, and between them the scene stands complete and terrible. Rehoboam, who might have kept the ten tribes with a gentle word, now cannot even hold the ground he stands on: his taskmaster is stoned to death before his eyes, and the king who had boasted of scorpions must flee for his life to Jerusalem. The little finger that was to be thicker than Solomon's loins has become a hand too weak to protect its own servant. Clarke, through Jarchi, supplies the provocation, the hated levy pressed on an angry people; Ellicott, through the versions, secures the officer's identity and the humiliating haste of the royal flight. The chronicler tells it soberly, in words all but identical to the older record, and lets the irony stand unremarked: the reign that opened with a threat of heavier burdens ends its first crisis with the king's own tribute-officer dead under a hail of stones and the king himself in headlong flight. Only the closing verse remains, to measure how lasting the breach would prove.
19:
The chapter ends on the permanence of the breach, "Israel rebelled against the house of David unto this day," and the two witnesses read the closing verse for its long echo and its hard lesson. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays the whole catastrophe at the king's own door. A few soft words, and the removal of part of the oppressive taxes, would have secured this people to the state and prevented the shedding of a sea of human blood that the separation of the kingdoms would cost. "Rehoboam was a fool; and through his folly he lost his kingdom." Nor is he the only such example on record: the Stuarts, Clarke observes, lost the realm of England in much the same way, while by a wiser mode of treatment the House of Brunswick continued to fill the British throne; and he closes, in his manner, with a verse of Virgil and a line of Horace on the ever-gliding stream of a well-founded fortune, and a fervent "Amen! Amen!"

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the arresting phrase "unto this day." Neither the compiler of Kings nor the chronicler, he notes, saw fit to alter an expression which had long since ceased to apply to the political circumstances of their own day, the northern kingdom having perished centuries before the chronicler wrote. The phrase is a fossil, carried over faithfully from an older source, like the similar time-worn notices Ellicott compares in the genealogies at 1 Chronicles 5:26. Its very survival is a small proof of the reverence with which the record was transmitted: a writer copying from Kings kept even a clause that no longer fit his times.

The witnesses close the chapter on its double note. Ellicott, from the letter, shows the phrase to be an inherited relic, evidence of the chronicler's fidelity to his source; Clarke, from the pulpit, drives home the moral that a kingdom was thrown away for want of a little kindness, and that the folly of Rehoboam has been repeated by princes ever since. "Israel rebelled against the house of David," and the breach never healed, the ten tribes never returning, until they were swept into an exile from which they did not come back. What opened with a reasonable petition at Shechem ends with a permanent schism in the people of God, and the chronicler records it plainly, that later ages might learn from a young king's pride how easily, and how lastingly, a great inheritance may be lost.
